coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe based on carbon quantum dots with spectral selectivity for sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in environmental waters
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yan
Chen, Guoqing
Ma, Chaoqun
Gu, Jiao
Yang, Taiqun
Li, Lei
Gao, Hui
Xiong, Yi
Zhu, Chun
Hu, Anqi
Li, Xin
Guan, Weinan
Zhang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid and l-Arginine as precursors. The synthesized CDs have excitation-dependent emission with emission peaks at 354 nm and 460 nm, corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelengths of 295 nm and 330 nm, respectively. CDs have good dispersion and resistance to photobleaching in aqueous solution, and can maintain stable fluorescence in highly concentrated salt environment. Based on the inner filter effect, Cr(VI) with strong absorption can shield the excitation light of CDs at 295 nm and absorb the emission fluorescence at 354 nm simultaneously. Due to the intermolecular electrostatic interaction, Hg(II) can coordinate with CDs surface groups and cause static quenching, which significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of CDs emitted at 460 nm. The linear ranges were 0.1–2 μM and 0.4–5 μM for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) detection, respectively, and limits of detection were 0.024 μM and 0.084 μM. Additionally, the probe was successfully applied to tap water and lake water samples. With high sensitivity and spectral selectivity, this sensing strategy realized the detection of two heavy metal ions by a single fluorescent probe, demonstrating its great potential in the field of environmental metal analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: carbon dot fluorescent probe
spectral selectivity
Cr(VI) ion detection
Hg(II) ion detection
inner filter effect
static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CDs
Selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) detection
Possible mechanism of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) sensing
Detections of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province [ KYCX22_2422 ] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [ 62375112 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of CDs at 10 nm; (b) Size distribution of CDs; (c) XRD image of CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of CDs and high resolution peaks of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s.
(a) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CDs; (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs and raw materials. Inset: aqueous solution of CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).

When the Cr(VI) concentration is 0–10 μM, (a) fluorescence spectra of the mixed system under excitation at 295 nm; (b) the relationship between the quenching degree (F0–F)/F0 and the concentration of Cr(VI); the inset shows the linear fitting curve between the degree of quenching and Cr(VI) concentration when the Cr(VI) concentration is 0.1–2 μM.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity of CDs after adding the same concentration of different metal ions; (b) Effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of other metal ions. (Ex = 295 nm).

When the concentration of Hg(II) is 0–20 μM, (a) Fluorescence spectra of the mixed system under the excitation at 330 nm; (b) Relationship between the quenching degree (F0–F)/F0 and the concentration of Hg(II); the inset shows the linear fitting curve when the concentration of Hg(II) is 0.4–5 μM.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity of CDs mixed with the same concentration of different metal ions; (b) Effect of Hg(II) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of other metal ions. (Ex = 330 nm).

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI), fluorescence excitation spectra (Em = 330 nm) and emission spectra (Ex = 295 nm) of CDs; (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, Cr(VI) and the mixture of both; (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs solutions before and after the addition of Cr(VI); (d) Zeta potential of CDs solutions before and after the addition of Cr(VI).

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, Hg(II) and the mixture of both; (b) Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs solution before and after the addition of Hg(II); (c) Zeta potential of CDs solution before and after the addition of Hg(II).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs, Cr(VI) detection and Hg(II) detection.
Results of the determination of Cr(VI) in real samples using CDs fluorescent probes (n = 3).
Results of the determination of Hg(II) in real samples using CDs fluorescent probes (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe based on carbon quantum dots with spectral selectivity for sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in environmental waters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Wuxi, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial optoelectronic engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid and l-Arginine as precursors. The synthesized CDs have excitation-dependent emission with emission peaks at 354 nm and 460 nm, corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelengths of 295 nm and 330 nm, respectively. CDs have good dispersion and resistance to photobleaching in aqueous solution, and can maintain stable fluorescence in highly concentrated salt environment. Based on the inner filter effect, Cr(VI) with strong absorption can shield the excitation light of CDs at 295 nm and absorb the emission fluorescence at 354 nm simultaneously. Due to the intermolecular electrostatic interaction, Hg(II) can coordinate with CDs surface groups and cause static quenching, which significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of CDs emitted at 460 nm. The linear ranges were 0.1–2 μM and 0.4–5 μM for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) detection, respectively, and limits of detection were 0.024 μM and 0.084 μM. Additionally, the probe was successfully applied to tap water and lake water samples. With high sensitivity and spectral selectivity, this sensing strategy realized the detection of two heavy metal ions by a single fluorescent probe, demonstrating its great potential in the field of environmental metal analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the development of national economy, especially the rapid development of industry, industrial wastewater discharge from metallurgy, mining, electroplating and other industries has increased greatly. As a result, the content of heavy metals in water bodies has increased dramatically, leading to severe water pollution caused by biotoxic heavy metal elements such as cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), lead (Pb), chromium (Cr) and metalloid arsenic (As) [1,2]. Among them, hexavalent chromium is a swallowed toxic/inhalation extremely toxic substance, which is very easily absorbed by human body and 100 times more toxic than trivalent chromium [3]. It can be absorbed by the skin, respiratory tract, or invade the body through the digestive tract or mucous membranes, causing human allergies, hereditary genetic defects, etc., and in severe cases can cause cancer [4–8]. It is a lasting danger to both the environment and humans. Mercury ions are also one of the most common and dangerous metal ions that can accumulate in living organisms and have a corrosive effect on the human digestive tract and damaging effects on the kidneys, capillaries and other organs. Prolonged exposure to high mercury levels can lead to brain damage and death in humans [9–13]. Therefore, it is important to establish some economical, convenient and sensitive methods for the detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II).

Carbon quantum dots, as an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials that have attracted much attention [14,15], have been widely used in various fields because of many advantages such as good biocompatibility, fluorescence stability, low toxicity, and easy availability of raw materials [16–20]. Several researchers have used carbon dot fluorescent probes for specific detections of Cr(VI) and Hg(II). Vaz et al. synthesized carbon dots by pyrolysis of citric acid and glutathione as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cr(VI) with a linear range of 0.10–12 μg/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.03 μg/mL [21]. Wang et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots by hydrothermal method using natural grapefruit juice and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources. Based on dynamic quenching, the probe showed good selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI). The LOD was 0.155 μM, and the linear range extended from 0 to 400 μM [22]. Omer et al. used xylose as a precursor to prepare extremely stable carbon dots whose fluorescence emission could be selectively quenched by Hg(II) with LOD as low as 10 nM [23]. Zhang et al. prepared carbon dots with good water solubility and fluorescence stability using N-aminoethylpiperazine and citric acid as precursors. Hg(II) could effectively burst the fluorescence of CDs, while the sensor could be applied to the detection of ions in river water and tap water [24]. So far, although many fluorescent probes based on carbon dots have been reported for the detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) or other heavy metal ions [25–27], it is not difficult to find that the vast majority of carbon dots used have only one effective emission center. Such carbon dot fluorescent probes have high specificity, but only one ion can be detected by one probe. There are almost no fluorescent probes that detect Cr(VI) and Hg(II) separately based on the spectral selectivity of carbon dots.

In this paper, spectrally selective fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method using 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid and l-Arginine as precursors and water as solvent with a quantum yield of 20 %. The carbon dots have two distinct emission centers with peak wavelengths of 354 nm and 460 nm, corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelengths of 295 nm and 330 nm, respectively. Based on inner filter effect, Cr(VI) can shield the excitation of CDs at 295 nm while absorbing the emission fluorescence at 354 nm. Due to intermolecular electrostatic interaction, Hg(II) can coordinate with carbon dot surface groups, which significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of CDs emitted at 460 nm. Thus, a method was developed to detect Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in tap water and lake water, respectively, by changing the excitation wavelength and utilizing the change in fluorescence intensity of different emission peaks at the same carbon dots (Scheme 1 ).

l-Arginine was purchased from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, AgNO3, MgCl2, BaCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, CaCl2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, KCl, NaCl, NaOH, HCl, H2SO4 and quinine sulfate were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hexavalent chromium standard was from Shanghai Bailingway Chemical Technology Co. All reagents and solvents used in the experiments were of analytical grade, and the experimental water was ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ-cm).

The morphological and lattice information of CDs were obtained by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images using JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was recorded by Frontier infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) maps of CDs were obtained by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS). The fluorescence spectra of all samples were measured using a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). To analyze the elemental composition of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were characterized by a UV-2600 UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured by an FLS920 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Zetasizer Nano ZS90 nanoparticle size potentiostat (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) was used to collect zeta potential.

2.2 mmol of l-Arginine and 2.6 mmol of 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid were dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water, shaken thoroughly for 5 min and sonicated at room temperature for 15 min to mix the mixture well. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL autoclave lined with PTFE and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After heating, the supernatant of the product after natural cooling to room temperature was centrifuged at 11000 r/min for 15 min. For purification, the centrifuged solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then dialyzed with a 1000 mw dialysis membrane for 24 h. Finally, the CDs were concentrated using a spinner and the prepared carbon dot concentrates were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for follow-up experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield of aqueous solutions of CDs was determined using a reference method. Quinine sulfate standard solution was obtained by dissolving quinine sulfate control in 0.1 M sulfuric acid. The excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate under this condition is known to be 55 %. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated by the following formula [28]:
Φ x
=
A s
I s
⋅
I x
A x
⋅
n x 2
n s 2
⋅
Φ s

Among them, Φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I is the integrated intensity (integrated area of the fluorescence spectrum under 365-nm excitation), n is the refractive index, and the subscripts of x and s represent the CDs solution and quinine sulfate solution, respectively. In order to make the results accurate, we prepared a series of CDs solution and control solution with different concentrations (concentrations adjusted so that the absorbance was in the range of 0–0.2) [29].

To investigate the fluorescence stability of CDs in salt environment, the change of fluorescence intensity was measured at NaCl concentration from 0 to 1.0 M. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at temperatures of 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, and 50 °C to analyze the influence of temperature on CDs. The CDs were continuously irradiated under a 365 nm UV lamp for 120 min, and the fluorescence intensity was measured every 15 min to analyze the resistance of CDs to photobleaching. A solution with pH 4–11 was configured using 0.1 M HCl solution and 0.1 M NaOH solution to study the effect of solution pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. All experiments were performed at two excitation wavelengths of 295 nm and 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) and Hg(II) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the selectivity of CDs for Cr(VI) and Hg(II), we selected 12 common interfering cations (Ba(II)、K(I)、Mg(II)、Cd(II)、Ca(II)、Fe(II)、Fe(III)、Cr(III)、Mn(II)、Pb(II)、Ag(I)、Cu(II)) and investigated the effects of these metal ions on CDs fluorescence. At room temperature, 10 μM of each of the 14 metal ions were mixed with CDs. The fluorescence spectra of the solutions were measured at 295 nm and 330 nm excitation, and the fluorescence intensities at 354 nm and 460 nm were recorded, respectively.

In a typical process, solutions of 4 times diluted CDs, different concentrations of Cr(VI) and pH 7 were mixed in the ratio of 1:1:8 by volume. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on the above samples after 40 min of reaction, in which the excitation wavelength was 295 nm and the emission wavelength was scanned in the range of 310–550 nm with an interval of 1 nm. The widths of both excitation and emission slits were set to 3 nm.

Similarly, solutions of 6 times diluted CDs, different concentrations of Hg(II) and pH 7 were mixed in the volume ratio of 1.5:1.5:7. After 10 min of reaction, the fluorescence spectra of the above samples were detected, where the excitation wavelength was 330 nm and the emission wavelength was scanned in the range of 340–650 nm with an interval of 1 nm. The widths of both excitation and emission slits were set to 5 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The limit of detection was calculated as LOD = 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation of three blank samples and s is the slope of the standard curve.

In order to assess the feasibility of applying the strategy to environmental waters, detections of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in tap water and lake water was carried out. The tap water samples were taken from our laboratory and the lake water samples were collected from our university lake. Before analysis, the lake water samples were centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min, and then both water samples were filtered three times using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, respectively. To calculate the recoveries based on this assay, different concentrations of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) standard solutions were added to the ambient water samples, respectively. All analytical methods were performed using the method described in Section 2.6. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

The morphology of the CDs was investigated by HRTEM. Fig. 1 (a) shows that the prepared CDs are nearly spherical and are dispersed. The average particle size of CDs is counted to be about 2.42 nm, and the particle size is mainly in the range of 1.5–3.6 nm (Fig. 1(b)). The physical phase and crystal structure of CDs in the range of 2θ = 0–90° were characterized using XRD. The results are shown in Fig. 1(c), where 2θ = 23.64° shows a strong diffraction peak which corresponded to the (002) plane of graphitic [30,31], indicating that the synthesized product has a typical amorphous carbon structure with relatively satisfactory crystallization properties [32]. The optical properties of CDs are affected by the structure of surface functional groups, so CDs were characterized by FTIR. Fig. 1(d) shows the peak at the high wave number of 3349 cm−1 corresponds to the N–H stretching vibration, indicating the presence of abundant –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs. The absorption at 3179 cm−1 attributes to the O–H stretching vibration. The peaks located at 1668 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1 wave numbers correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO and COO− in the carboxyl group. The bands at 1550 cm−1 is owing to the CC stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1159 cm−1 can be attributed to C–O, C–N. In addition, the absorption peak at low wave number of 794 cm−1 is due to the C–H stretching vibration. These results indicate that large quantity of functional groups, such as –NH2 and –COOH, exist on the CDs surface, making CDs water solubility and reactive.

The surface structure of CDs was further analyzed based on the XPS measurement spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the XPS maps indicate that the CDs have three different bond energy peaks located at 285.12 eV, 400.11 eV, and 532.11 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. This indicates that the CDs are composed of C, N, and O elements, accounting for 66.61 %, 8.73 %, and 24.66 %, respectively. The high-resolution energy spectrum of each element of CDs was peak fitted. C1s (Fig. 2(b)) is divided into three peaks, with peaks of 284.78 eV corresponding to sp2C (C–C/CC), and 286.38 eV and 288.48 eV corresponding to C–N/C–O and O–CO, respectively. The high-resolution energy spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2(c)) can be fitted to two peaks, located at 399.98 eV and 401.68 eV, which prove the existence of C–N and N–H. Reformulating the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2(d)), there are three peaks, namely CO at 531.08 eV, C–O at 532.28 eV and O–H at 533.28 eV. The characterization results of XPS and FTIR spectra are mutually provable.

Next, we analyzed the optical properties of CDs and measured the three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of raw materials and CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CDs shows excitation-dependent emission of CDs. When the excitation wavelength is 295 nm, the CDs have fluorescence peaks at both 354 nm and 460 nm, but the fluorescence intensity at 354 nm is stronger. And the optimal excitation corresponding to the emission peak at 460 nm is 330 nm, as shown in the normalized excitation emission spectra in Fig. S1 (a) and (b). The UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3(b) shows that l-Arginine (blue line) has no significant absorption peak in the 200–425 nm wavelength range, and 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (red line) has strong absorption at 240 nm and 310 nm. The synthesized CDs (black line) have a wider absorption band at 275–325 nm with a peak of about 295 nm, which may be related to the n-π* jump [33]. Compared to the raw material, the peak at 240 nm almost disappears and the position of the absorption peak at 310 nm changes by about 15 nm, which are both connected with the surface state of the newly synthesized CDs. The aqueous solution of CDs is a brownish-yellow liquid (inset of Fig. 3(b)) in daylight (left picture) and emits a strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (right picture). In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was determined to be 20 % with quinine sulfate solution adopted as the standard; the experimental data and results are shown in Fig. S2.

A series of experiments were performed to study the fluorescence stability of CDs for practical application, including the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs in salt solution, the resistance of CDs to photobleaching, the effect of temperature and pH on the CDs fluorescence stability. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the peak fluorescence intensities corresponding to both excitation wavelengths remain almost constant as the concentration of NaCl is raised from 0 to 1.0 mol/L. The phenomenon suggests that the CDs are found to be stable in the high salt environment. Fig. S3(b) shows that the CDs fluorescence intensity remains relatively stable overall when continuously irradiated by UV lamps, although there is a slight fluctuation at 295 nm excitation. Fig. S3(c) shows that the fluorescence intensity of both peaks decrease with increasing temperature. This is because as the temperature of the solution increases, the molecular motion speeds up and the probability of intermolecular collisions increases, leading to an increase in the non-radiative leap rate. However, fluorescence is the result of the radiative leap and therefore the fluorescence intensity decreases [34]. This also indicates that it is most appropriate to conduct the experiments at 20 °C in order to maintain higher fluorescence intensity of CDs. When CDs are added to solutions of different pH values in Fig. S3(d), the fluorescence intensity decreases in strong acid or strong base solutions, which may be due to deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of CDs [28,35] or damage to the emission sites [36]. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity is more stable at pH 5–9, and it is appropriate to choose solution of pH 7 to adjust the pH value of the working solution for subsequent experiments. In summary, it can be demonstrated that CDs have good fluorescence stabilities and can be available in detecting Cr(VI) and Hg(II).

In order to obtain more accurate detection results, some experimental conditions were optimized, including the concentration of CDs and reaction time. As shown in Fig. S4(a), the CDs stock solution was diluted to 2, 4, 6 and 8 times, and equal amounts of deionized water and 10 μM Cr(VI) were added to each part. Then the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured and the quenching degree was calculated. The results show that the quenching degree is greatest when the CDs stock solution is diluted 4 times. Fig. S4(b) indicates that the quenching degree remains almost constant when the system is reacted for 35 min, so 40 min is subsequently chosen as the reaction time.

The sensitivity detection of Cr(VI) by CDs fluorescent probe in water was carried out under optimal conditions. After adding 0–10 μM of Cr(VI) to the CDs solution, at the excitation wavelength of 295 nm,the fluorescence intensity of the blended solution decreases gradually (Fig. 4 (a)), and the quenching degree exhibits a pattern of a rapid rise followed by a slow (Fig. 4(b)). Cr(VI) at 10 μM causes a fluorescence burst of CDs by nearly 75 %. When the concentration of Cr (VI) added is in the 0.1–2 μM range, a good linear relationship between CDs fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cr(VI) added is observed (inset of Fig. 4(b)). The linear equation obtained by fitting is (F0–F)/F0 = 0.25737[C]+0.10638, where F0 and F are the peak intensity at 354 nm in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) in the mixed solution separately and [C] is the concentration of Cr(VI). The calculated R2 is 0.997 and LOD is 0.024 μM. Comparing our results with other methods applying carbon dot fluorescence probes for Cr(VI) detection, the results are shown in Table S1. Our detection method is able to show superior sensitivity in the lower concentration range.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the method for Cr(VI) ions, the fluorescence responses of CDs to 14 common metal ions of the same concentration were measured and competition experiments were performed with an excitation wavelength of 295 nm. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), it is clear that Cr(VI) can cause the maximum quenching of fluorescence emitted by CDs at 354 nm when CDs are incubated with multiple metal ions, separately. When other metal ions co-exist with Cr(VI) in Fig. 5(b), Cr(VI) is still able to efficiently quench CDs fluorescence, and the degree is almost not affected by other metal ions. The above show the remarkable selectivity and anti-interference ability of the detection method for Cr(VI) ions.

First, the optimal dilution times and reaction time for the detection of Hg(II) by CDs were determined. In Fig. S5(a), when the CDs stock solution is diluted 6 times, the fluorescence quenching is greatest. The fluorescence intensity of CDs in Fig. S5(b) decreases sharply within 1 min, slowly decreases within 1–5 min, and basically stabilizes after 5 min. Therefore, it was tentatively considered that the reaction was complete at 5 min. But for the stability of the assay, 10 min was taken as the optimal detection time.

Next, sensitivity assays were performed under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), after adding 0–20 μM of Hg(II) to CDs solution, at 330 nm excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution diminishes gradually with increasing concentration of Hg(II) added, and 20 μM of Hg(II) can burst the fluorescence of CDs by nearly 70 %. The peak intensities at 460 nm were taken to calculate the relationship between the degree of quenching and the concentration of Hg(II) in Fig. 6(b). It is found that there is a linear relationship between the two when the concentration of Hg(II) is 0.4–5 μM (inset of Fig. 6(b)). The linear equation is (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0679[C]+0.1809, where F0 and F are the peak intensities at 460 nm in the absence and presence of Hg(II), [C] is the concentration of Hg(II), R2 is 0.992 and the calculated LOD is 0.084 μM. The results were compared to some of the current literature in the field of Hg (II) detection, and the results are shown in Table S2. The strategy we created is almost able to reach the best detection level available. In summary, our method is able to detect Cr(VI) and Hg(II) separately by changing the excitation wavelength of the same probe, and on this basis, we obtain excellent specificity and sensitivity for both.

Moreover, the fluorescence responses of CDs to 14 common metal ions were still measured and competition experiments were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. As Fig. 7 (a) shows, at 460 nm emission, Cr(VI), Cd(II) and Cu(II) have a slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, but comparison shows that the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Hg(II) is most obvious. In addition, when other metal ions co-exist with Hg(II), Hg(II) is still able to efficiently burst the fluorescence (Fig. 7(b)), indicating that CDs have relatively high selectivity for Hg(II). In summary, although the same carbon dot fluorescent probe was used to detect Cr(VI) and Hg(II), the difference in detection conditions such as the optimum excitation wavelength and the optimal concentration of CDs between the two made the sensing strategy excellent in spectral selectivity. The two metal ions were able to achieve mutual non-interference between them under their respective optimal detection conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the mechanism by which CDs fluorescence change as a result of Cr (VI) and Hg(II), a series of measurements such as absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, and zeta potentials of CDs before and after the addition of Cr (VI) or Hg(II) had been carried out.

First, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) was compared with the fluorescence spectra of CDs. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), it is obvious that the absorption peak of Cr(VI) in the 230 nm–310 nm range partially overlaps with the excitation peak of CDs at 295 nm, while the extensive UV absorption in the range of 320 nm–400 nm almost completely overlaps with the emission peak of CDs at 354 nm. Fluorescence quenching induced by either fluorescence resonance energy transfer [37] or a strong inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and Cr(VI) [38] may exhibit this significant spectral overlap. Next, the absorption spectra of CDs were measured before and after the addition of Cr(VI), and the results are shown in Fig. 8(b). Although the absorption spectrum of the CDs/Cr(VI) system (blue line) has a large change compared to that of CDs (black line), it is nearly the same as the sum of the values of those of CDs and Cr(VI) (purple line). There is no significant difference in intensity and position of the absorption peaks, indicating that no formation of the ground state complexes, i.e., no static quenching [39]. After the addition of Cr(VI), the fluorescence lifetime of CDs changes from 1.41 ns to 1.52 ns in Fig. 8(c), a change of only 0.11 ns. Although the change is very small, the possibility of the existence of dynamic quenching cannot be completely excluded [40]. Finally, the Zeta potential of CDs is measured to be −2.94 mV and that of the CDs/Cr(VI) to be −1.43 mV (Fig. 8(d)), with very small changes, suggesting that coordination between Cr(VI) and CDs does not occur [41]. In summary, a very small fraction of the relevant features of the dynamic quenching were measured, but we believe that the detection mechanism of CDs for Cr(VI) is mainly an inner filter effect. Cr(VI) with strong absorption could shield the excitation of CDs and absorb the emission fluorescence of CDs at the same time, which ensured the efficient occurrence of IFE and made a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

Similarly, as shown in Fig. 9 (a), Hg(II) used in the experiments has no significant absorption peak in the measurement range, while the position of the absorption peak of CDs is significantly red-shifted from 295 nm to 305 nm after the addition of Hg(II). The change indicates that an interaction between CDs and Hg(II) occurred, most likely due to the formation of CDs/Hg(II) ground state complex and the static quenching [42]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs changes from 5.38 ns to 5.03 ns after the addition of Hg(II) (Fig. 9(b)). And the Zeta potential of the CDs/Hg(II) system in Fig. 9(c) is 25.97 mV, which is a significant increase compared to that of CDs. There was a strong affinity between functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and Hg(II), and the significant change in Zeta potential indicated that the coordination between Hg(II) and CDs surface groups was likely attributed to intermolecular electrostatic interactions [41,43]. In short, the process of dynamic quenching may have little contribution to detection, and the experimental results as a whole are more in line with the characteristics of static quenching [11,44]. Therefore, static quenching between Hg(II) and CDs is the main reason for the change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

In conclusion, both Cr(VI) and Hg(II) can cause the prepared probe to exhibit spectral manifestations of reduced fluorescence intensity. However, due to the differences in absorption and reaction conditions between the two metals, different detection mechanisms are ultimately obtained, and the probe exhibits corresponding spectral selectivity. Moreover, Cr(VI) and Hg(II), as heavy metal ions, more or less dynamically quench the fluorescence of CDs, which are rich in various functional groups on the surface [45], but this process does not contribute much to the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summation, we successfully synthesized fluorescent carbon dots with spectral selectivity and a quantum yield of 20 % for efficient and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II) using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Due to IFE, the excitation of CDs at 295 nm could be shielded and the corresponding emission at 354 nm was absorbed by Cr(VI). The LOD was measured as low as 0.024 μM with a linear range of 0.1–2 μM based on the variation of the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Meanwhile, Hg(II) could coordinate with functional groups on the surface of CDs to form CDs/Hg(II) ground state complexes, resulting in a significant reduction of the fluorescence emission of CDs at 460 nm. The LOD for Hg(II) was as low as 0.084 μM with a linear range of 0.4–5 μM. Acceptable recovery rates ranging from 96.3 % to 103.3 % suggested that the method was still feasible for practical applications. Overall, this is the first time that the spectral selectivity of the same carbon dot fluorescent probe has been utilized for the separate detection of Cr(VI) and Hg(II), and its simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity make the method promising for environmental water quality monitoring.
This work was supported by the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province [KYCX22_2422] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China [62375112].

Yan Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Guoqing Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chaoqun Ma: Resources. Jiao Gu: Resources. Taiqun Yang: Validation. Lei Li: Supervision. Hui Gao: Data curation. Yi Xiong: Writing – review & editing. Chun Zhu: Investigation. Anqi Hu: Visualization. Xin Li: Visualization. Weinan Guan: Visualization. Wei Zhang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
